speaker: interviewer
text: Here, we're on tape number three. When we stopped the last tape, we're talking about how you and your mother were managing alone without your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think my mother had some money, my parent must have had some money. And I actually remember that they had some dollars, some gold dollars. And she must have been selling it. And we were buying, you know, what we would eat in the ghetto, what was the biggest food, was we would have bread and we would have potatoes. And I don't even-- don't think we had any milk. I don't remember having any milk. I certainly don't remember having any meat. Maybe in the beginning, I think, when my father was still working, you know, in the beginning of the ghetto, when he was still going out to the store. But once he couldn't go out anymore-- most people couldn't go out, except those people that work for Oskar Schindler, for Madritsch, and for Optima. Those were factories that people would go and work and were able to have contact with Polish workers. And those people were able to buy some food and smuggle it into the ghetto for their families. But the rest of the people had very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know people then who were working for Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really didn't know any people, because when people went to work for Oskar Schindler in the beginning, a lot of people did not want to go and work for a factory outside of the ghetto because they were afraid that they would leave in the morning, they would come back at night, their families would be arrested, they would be taken out of the ghetto on trucks. Some people were afraid. But within a very short time, most families had broken up. Most mothers were taken away, fathers were taken away, children were taken away. And if the parents were taken away, there were so many orphans. And also, somehow, from the other towns outside of Cracow, the little towns that were being liquidated, sometimes only children were brought back. Those children were in the orphans' home in the ghetto. And some older people were just taking care of them in the orphans' home. So food was very hard to get, and people were getting very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick. I had pneumonia three times. And I remember one of my cousins brought me lemon because, you know, it's vitamin C and we didn't have any medication.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any medical attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were still doctors in the ghetto. There was a hospital in the ghetto. And, again, my parents knew a lot of the people, a lot of the-- they had a lot of friends. They lived in Cracow all their life. They had a large family so they knew everybody. I mean, if it was somebody that wasn't a friend of my mother's, it would be a friend of her older sister or her older brothers, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think that because they had a lot of connections, that they knew so many people, people were sort of helping each other. But it was very meager-- meager food, meager rations. You had tried very hard to have a piece of bread last you at least for a day. Were you and your mother still working in the paper factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were working in the paper factory. And I think that we were not on the same shift because of the little girl, of my little cousin. And we couldn't leave her alone and we had to-- so somebody, one of us wanted to be with her. So we were not working on the same shift. And, of course, very shortly after my father was taken away, the ghetto was going to be liquidated. And the ghetto was being liquidated because we were building a concentration camp outside of the Cracow ghetto, which was called Plaszow. And that was a very-- I think about seven kilometers from the ghetto, not a very long walk. And was a small village, and there were Jewish cemeteries under the village. And so people-- we had to go and build roads. They broke-- we had to break up the tombstone and crush it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do this while you were still in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did that while I was in the ghetto towards the end, towards the end of it, what they were already liquidating the workshops in the ghetto, because there were a lot of workshops in the ghetto. The workshops were being moved to the camp. So while they were being moved, there was no place for us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked to Plaszow and we would sit and-- the women-- women, we used to build the roads. We were sitting building the road, breaking up the tombstones. I remember whole row of women breaking up the tombstone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the commander of the camp was Amon Goth. He was the one who would be in charge of liquidation of the ghetto as soon as the camp was ready, the barracks. The men build the barracks. And the Germans came very early in the morning, about 4:30 in the morning, and they were running through the ghetto. The truck was driving through the ghetto with loud speakers, that everybody had to pack up and leave the ghetto by 6 o'clock at night. Everybody had to go to their place of work. And you would work with the, you know, it was the gemeinschaft, it was the workshop people. And anybody under 12, children under 12, had to stay in the ghetto in the orphans' home and you couldn't take children. And my mother and I, we pack a little suitcase for each one. And I took my cousin and went to the corner to meet my workshop. And, you know, they wouldn't let me. The Germans and the Jewish police, they says, you can't take her. She doesn't have any paper. You have to take her to the-- leave her in the orphans' home. And I remember somebody said to me, why are you so worried. She will be OK. Look at all the people, all the children in the orphans' home. So I took her by her hand. And she was such a pretty little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, Rena?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Jenny. And she was-- you know, she was blonde eyes-- I mean, blonde hair, blue eyes, smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ulrich. Jenny Ulrich. And so I took her to the orphans' home. And there were a lot of-- hundreds and hundreds of orphans. And I remember one of the women who was a friend of my mother and she was staying there with her two children, and she says you don't have to worry. I will take care of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left her. There were, I know, people all day long, even the day before, all I could-- all people talked about is if they could just find somebody to give the children to. They wanted to give the children to Polish people, too. What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was March 13, 1943. And I ran to the corner to meet my group, my mother was already either behind me or ahead of me. I don't know because I actually walked without my mother. I was afraid of what happened to my mother. I don't know where my mother was. But we-- you know, we walked and I walked with the people from my workshop. And there was a couple there that had a little boy. I think he was maybe two. And his mother had him on her-- in her backpack. They smuggled him. They drugged him, put him in a backpack, carried him to Plaszow. And because we were not searched when we came in-- there was so many people, you know, so many hundred of people walking in. And it was getting late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was not with me for a while. I don't-- I remember just walking with this couple and thinking that they have this little boy, the little boy is there. And what are they going to do with him? But as we were going, walking by outside of the ghetto wall, and it was getting dark-- it was already night. You know, it was March, no daylight saving time. It was dark already, cold, snowing, miserable weather. And the dogs are always barking. The dogs always ready to jump and attack you, tear you to pieces. And Germans always yelling and screaming [GERMAN]. You know, get out. Hurry up. And we started to hear shooting in the ghetto. And we were running, we were already not walking. We had to run. They were running, made us run. And they were shooting in the ghetto already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got to the camp, and then we went to the barrack. And I think that that's where my mother was, that I finally found my mother in the barrack. I really don't remember her walking with me to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And camp, you know, the barracks were huge and very crowded. The bunk beds, they were really not like bunk bed. They were like shelves. You couldn't sit on it. You had to lay down. Just the way you see in pictures, that's really the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people on a-- on a level, on each level?

speaker: interviewee
text: On a level? There were like three to each one. Probably maybe 35, 40 on walls like this, three tiers high. Barracks were, you know, freezing cold in wintertime. There was a stove in the middle, never really could warm the whole place. And, of course, everybody was so petrified. We were so afraid of what was going to happen. And the next day, some of those people who work for Oskar Schindler or went to work for Madritsch, those were the people who were able to smuggle their children, that had already made arrangement with their Polish friends and smuggled the children and gave them to the Polish people, their friends who would-- that to agree to help them, to take care, to save their children. There was a group from the camp that went to clean up the ghetto that day, the first day of camp. The first day in the camp, you know, it was the first time men and women were separated. The barracks were men barracks, women barracks. And there was a bathhouse, there was a latrine, there was a kitchen, and there was a [NON-ENGLISH]. It's like a clinic. And the workshops were up on a hill. And up on a hill was the villa of Amon Goth. And in the middle was this huge appellplatz, were like a huge meadow where everybody had to gather every morning and every night and be counted, make sure nobody escaped. So we were counted by the Jewish police, by the Ukrainians. Ukrainians actually volunteered to guard, to be guards. And then, of course, the Germans. And then they went, reported to Amon Goth. And Amon Goth would, you know, he would stand on that appellplatz. And every morning, he would decide that he has to teach us a lesson, so that we would be either witnesses to public hangings or executions or people just would be taken out and just beaten. I mean, a real sadistic killer, a real sadistic killer. Just enjoyed killing innocent people. And it was that first day, that first-- the end of the day, the first day, when those who were sent to clean the ghetto came back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the ghetto, little children were murdered. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some of the children were just thrown from the roof, the little ones. And the other-- some of the people were-- and children-- were put on trucks and sent to Auschwitz. Sam, the head of the Jewish police, with his wife and children-- one of his daughter was married, she was pregnant-- but 60 people were brought to Plaszow and were killed. Right up on another hill in camp, that's where they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they hiding in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: They remained because they were supposed to liquidate it. And after it was all liquidated, they thought-- maybe some of them thought they would save themselves because they carried the orders of the Germans. But, of course, once they did what they were told to do, they were brought and, you know, shot at the edge of the hill over there. A lot of people were. And it was then that we realized nothing was going to really save us, nobody was going to save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you talked about in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't imagine. I think people still always had hope. The war was going on and the German would lose, but the Germans were winning everywhere and making steps. And just couldn't imagine what was going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what really happened was that we were so devastated and were always so hungry that the matter of survival became a matter of hours, you know? Like you get up in the morning and you hoped you would have a little-- enough breakfast to last you through lunch, and that lunch last you through the night, and that the night will pass without any patrols coming into the barracks, and you would get up in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a young boy in camp and he would wake us up every morning with-- he played on a trumpet. 4:30 in the morning, I think, we had to get up. By 5:30, I think, we had to stand already on the appellplatz. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, even-- even in the workshops, they would come and take people. They would decide. They would have a selection. They needed people. They would take people out to another camp. I was working this paper gemeinschaft. And I was working at a table. I work at different machines and on the table where we were cutting up the-- putting the notebooks together and I was working. All girls, all of girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother working there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was working there too, but it was like the girls. And one of the girls became a well-known writer and she was talking-- we were all making up poems.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't think right this minute. I have her-- I have her books. Karmel. Ilona Karmel. She wrote And we used to make up dream of surviving and make up poems. And one night, they came, the Germans came, and they took her and her sister and her mother. And her sister was married, and she cried so, and she begged the guards to let her stay with her husband or to say goodbye to her husband. And they beat her so and they took her-- took them away. And, I mean, she was standing right next to me. And I was very lucky. I was just very lucky that I escaped a lot of the beating. If somebody was-- when they took those girls away right sitting next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think they didn't pick you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lucky. I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work in the paper factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in the paper factory until-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until the end of 1943, when, again, my mother heard that Oskar Schindler wanted to have more people come to work in his factory. And by then, already, Oskar Schindler's workers lived in the camp which he built next to the factory. And so she send me to Marcel Goldberg, who was in charge of the list. And my mother and I were put on a list and we went to work for Oskar Schindler. Now, I remember I asked him to take also for my aunt, but he did not. My mother's younger sister was still-- she work in a fur factory, a furrier. They were making furs. So but my mother and I went to work for Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did Marcel Goldberg put you on the list and not--

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't. I guess he still felt some sense of obligation because of my father for me and my mother. And my grandfather also went to Schindler's factory because my other uncle was able to arrange it. I had an uncle, my grandfather had a daughter, only daughter among eight boys. So her husband also was working for Oskar Schindler, and they were able to get my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, my father's mother, died the first day in Plaszow. We were lucky that she died because she was-- she was in the barracks. And when they started to call that there was an appellplatz, and everybody had to go out and march to the appellplatz, she died of a heart attack. See, she saved herself so much, so much, because my youngest uncle was killed like the second day while we were in the camp. He was a painter. He was painting the barracks. And Amon Goth's standing yards, hundreds of yards away from him, just shot him for no reason. No reason at all. And my grandfather was there. And his two sons were there. Shot him. So she saved herself that. And one of my cousins, a young cousin, I think he must have been maybe 18, maybe 20 at the time, he was killed by Amon Goth together with an uncle of his and a group of people that worked for Amon Goth in the camp. He was-- like he was the director of the Jewish police in the camp, the uncle. And my cousin worked for him. He gave him a job. And those people, again, they were accused of trying to escape. They were killed. They were shot right in front of our eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were these people's names, these relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin was Moniek Ferber. Such a nice, good-looking boy. And we had to-- we had to stand there and watch, walk around to see them dead. And we had to stand by while they were being burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: They burned them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They burned them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to stand-- everybody. The whole camp. And especially the family, my aunt, mother. For nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you started to work for Oskar Schindler--

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we went to work for Oskar Schindler, it was like, you know, going to heaven, because we were away from Amon Goth. We were away from all this killing. And at Oskar Schindler's camp, of course, we had more food. We had-- the barracks were small. It was warmer in winter. In summer, when it was very hot, we were allowed to sleep outside. I remember, nights, we just slept outside on blankets because the barracks, you know, we couldn't breathe. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think of escaping or running away?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place to escape. There was no way for us to escape because you had to have connection, you have to know people on the outside. You had to have money and you had to have the courage. You had to look Aryan, which we didn't. I may not-- in United States, it's probably hard to say who looks Aryan and who doesn't. But in Europe, in Poland, Poles knew exactly who was Aryan and who was Jewish, even if the Germans didn't know. And, of course, it was dangerous, because if you escaped and the Poles would find you, they would report you to the police and you would be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but there were people who tried to escape. There were people who tried to hide. There were young people who escaped and joined working the Underground in the woods, in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they not only had to hide from the Germans, they had to hide from the Poles. They had to hide from the Polish Underground because the Polish Underground would hunt the Jewish Underground fighters just like the Germans would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we just-- we were to-- my mother was alone with a child. I was not really a child anymore and I would never think of leaving my mother and doing anything without her, so-- and us, of course, we were still working for Oskar Schindler. It was wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler was-- he was handsome, very handsome, well-dressed. And just looking at him, you know, just looking at him made you feel good, made you feel hopeful. He made you feel good. He made people feel-- his workers feel that he cared about us and he wanted to help us and he wanted to feed us so we wouldn't starve. He would fight for us. If the Germans wanted to kill us, he would try to intervene. He would-- he was just wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt safe. I felt safe. I felt protected. We all did. I mean, he would do things like-- on a night shift, he would say that he needs some young women to come and clean the office. So we would go-- there would be group of us, maybe six of us would go up to clean the office. And we were all young, but I was one of the youngest. Everybody was a year or two or three or four years older than me. And once we were in the office, we would lay under a desk and sleep. One person would stand guard to make sure the guards would not come up. I mean, to be able to sleep on a night shift, that was-- gave you strength, a chance to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know that you were sleeping?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, he knew. Of course he knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you-- what was this factory making? What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, part of the factory was making pots and pans. This is what they were making before the war. And when he took the factory over, this is what they were making. They added mesquite and other thing. But the other part of the factory he converted for making ammunition. And I was working on that part that was making ammunition. Again, I was lucky because the parts that was making pots and pans was very hard work because you had to dip those heavy pots and pans in very, very hot water or something. So it was so unbearable hot over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was working in the factory on a little machine, a lathe. And I was making shells for the bullets. And I liked it. I like actually doing it, you know? What was the name of this factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emalia. Emalia factory. And then, of course, when the time came that we-- you know, he had to liquidate the factory, move the factory to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll stop now and go on to the next tape.
